covered_sections: 2.2 Ionic Bonds>2.2.4 Properties of Ionic Compounds (Experiments)
2.3 Covalent Bonds>2.3.2 Intermolecular Forces (Dipole-Dipole, Hydrogen Bonding, London Forces)

mini_headings_covered: Lattice Energies from the Born-Haber Cycle

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following substances exhibits dipole-dipole forces as the dominant intermolecular force?
questions.hint: Dipole-dipole forces occur in polar molecules.
questions.explanation: HCl is a polar molecule with a permanent dipole due to electronegativity difference. F₂ and CH₄ are nonpolar (only London forces). He is monatomic and nonpolar (only London forces).

source_trace.content_anchor: Dipole-dipole forces act between the molecules possessing permanent dipole

explanations_tiered.definition: Dipole-dipole forces are intermolecular attractions between polar molecules.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: These forces arise from the partial positive end of one molecule attracting the partial negative end of another.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Polar molecules have a positive side and a negative side, like magnets, so they stick together. Nonpolar molecules don't have this.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: HCl has a permanent dipole (Hδ⁺–Clδ⁻). The δ⁺ end of one HCl attracts the δ⁻ end of another, creating dipole-dipole forces. F₂ and CH₄ are nonpolar.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Dipole-dipole forces are stronger than London forces for molecules of comparable mass but weaker than hydrogen bonding. They significantly affect boiling points.
reasoning_steps: Determine if each molecule is polar: F₂ (nonpolar), HCl (polar), CH₄ (nonpolar), He (nonpolar).
Polar molecules exhibit dipole-dipole forces.
HCl is the only polar molecule listed.
misconceptions: All molecules have dipole-dipole forces. False; only polar molecules do.

glossary_entry.definition: Intermolecular attractions between permanent dipoles of polar molecules.

flashcard.front: What type of intermolecular force exists in HCl?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What intermolecular forces are present in HCl?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Dipole-dipole forces and London dispersion forces.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with London forces in nonpolar molecules.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. HCl is polar and exhibits dipole-dipole forces; the others are nonpolar and only have London forces.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Why is the molecular shape of ammonia (\(\mathrm{NH_3}\)) trigonal pyramidal rather than tetrahedral?

options.A: Nitrogen does not hybridize its orbitals.
options.B: The lone pair on nitrogen occupies one vertex of the tetrahedron, pushing the N–H bonds into a pyramid.
options.C: The N–H bonds are ionic, not covalent.
options.D: Ammonia has resonance structures that flatten the molecule.

questions.hint: Count electron groups around N: three bonding pairs, one lone pair.
questions.explanation: NH₃ has four electron groups (tetrahedral electron geometry). One group is a lone pair, so the molecular shape is trigonal pyramidal (like a tetrahedron missing one vertex). The lone pair repels the bonding pairs, giving a bond angle of about 107°.

source_trace.content_anchor: The overall arrangement of four electron pairs is tetrahedral. But in NH3 one of the electron pairs is a lone pair, so the geometry of NH3 is trigonal pyramidal.

explanations_tiered.definition: Trigonal pyramidal geometry has a central atom with three bonded atoms and one lone pair.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Four electron groups arrange tetrahedrally. The lone pair occupies one position, and the three hydrogens occupy the other three, giving a pyramid shape.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a three-legged stool: the legs are the hydrogens, and the seat is the lone pair pushing down. That's trigonal pyramidal.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: NH₃ has 4 electron groups (3 bonds, 1 lone pair). Electron geometry is tetrahedral, but molecular shape is trigonal pyramidal because lone pairs are not counted as atoms.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The lone pair exerts greater repulsion than bonding pairs, compressing the H–N–H angle from 109.5° to 107°. This is predicted by VSEPR and confirmed by experiment.
reasoning_steps: Draw Lewis structure of NH₃: N with 3 bonds + 1 lone pair.
Count electron groups = 4 → tetrahedral electron geometry.
One group is a lone pair → molecular shape = trigonal pyramidal.
misconceptions: NH₃ is tetrahedral. False; tetrahedral is electron geometry, but molecular shape is trigonal pyramidal.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: NH₃: 4 e⁻ groups (1 lone pair) → trigonal pyramidal.

glossary_entry.definition: Molecular shape with three bonded atoms and one lone pair on central atom.

flashcard.front: What is the molecular shape of NH₃?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Trigonal pyramidal (tetrahedral electron geometry with one lone pair).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 4 e⁻ groups, one lone pair occupies a tetrahedral vertex, leaving three H atoms in a pyramid.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Shape with three bonds and one lone pair.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The lone pair occupies one tetrahedral position, leaving three H atoms in a trigonal pyramidal arrangement.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which molecule has a net dipole moment?
questions.hint: Look for an asymmetric molecule with polar bonds that do not cancel.
questions.explanation: CH₂Cl₂ has a tetrahedral shape with two H and two Cl atoms. The C–Cl bonds are polar, and the arrangement is not symmetric enough to cancel all bond dipoles, resulting in a net dipole moment. CO₂, CCl₄, and BF₃ are symmetric with dipole cancellation.

source_trace.content_anchor: CHCl3 has a significant dipole moment

explanations_tiered.definition: A net dipole moment occurs when bond dipoles do not cancel due to molecular asymmetry.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Symmetry can cancel bond dipoles even if individual bonds are polar. Asymmetric distribution of polar bonds leads to a net dipole.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a molecule is like a tug-of-war with equal pulls in opposite directions, the net is zero. CH₂Cl₂ has uneven pulls, so it has a net dipole.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: CO₂ (linear) and CCl₄ (tetrahedral, identical atoms) have symmetric bond dipole cancellation. BF₃ (trigonal planar) also cancels. CH₂Cl₂ has different atoms (H vs Cl) breaking symmetry, giving a net dipole.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The vector sum of bond dipoles in CH₂Cl₂ is nonzero because the C–Cl dipole magnitude is greater than C–H, and the tetrahedral geometry does not allow perfect cancellation.
reasoning_steps: Determine molecular geometry for each.
Identify if identical terminal atoms are present.
If terminal atoms are not all identical, symmetry may be broken.
CH₂Cl₂: tetrahedral with two different terminal atoms → net dipole.
misconceptions: All tetrahedral molecules are nonpolar. False; if terminal atoms differ, can be polar (CH₂Cl₂, CHCl₃).

glossary_entry.definition: Measure of molecular polarity; vector sum of bond dipoles.

flashcard.front: Which is polar: CCl₄ or CH₂Cl₂?
flashcard.flashcard_back: CH₂Cl₂ is polar; CCl₄ is nonpolar due to symmetry.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Polar; the tetrahedral arrangement with different atoms (H and Cl) prevents dipole cancellation.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show vector addition for CH₂Cl₂ vs CCl₄.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Predict molecular polarity from shape and bond polarity
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. CH₂Cl₂ has an asymmetric tetrahedral arrangement with different atoms, leading to a net dipole moment.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: How does the hardness of diamond compare to that of graphite, and what bonding difference explains this?

options.A: Diamond is softer; it has weak layers.
options.B: Graphite is harder; it has strong covalent bonds in all directions.
options.C: Diamond is harder; it has a three-dimensional network of strong covalent bonds.
options.D: Both are equally hard because both are carbon.

questions.hint: Consider the bonding in diamond (sp³) vs graphite (sp² with layers).
questions.explanation: Diamond has a three-dimensional network of strong covalent σ bonds (sp³). This rigid structure makes diamond extremely hard. Graphite has strong covalent bonds within layers (sp²) but weak London forces between layers, allowing layers to slide, making it soft.

source_trace.content_anchor: diamond and graphite are examples of network covalent solids

explanations_tiered.definition: Network covalent solids have atoms covalently bonded throughout the entire crystal.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Diamond (sp³) has all bonds in 3D → extremely hard. Graphite (sp²) has strong in-plane bonds but weak interlayer forces → soft and slippery.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Diamond is like a 3D jungle gym of strong sticks — very hard. Graphite is like a stack of paper sheets: each sheet is strong, but sheets slide apart easily.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In diamond, each C is sp³ hybridized, forming four σ bonds to neighboring C atoms in a rigid 3D lattice. In graphite, C atoms are sp² hybridized, forming hexagonal layers; layers are held by London forces.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The hardness of diamond (Mohs 10) is due to strong directional covalent bonds with high bond energy (347 kJ/mol) in all directions. Graphite (Mohs 1–2) has weak interlayer van der Waals forces (~10 kJ/mol).
reasoning_steps: Recall diamond: all atoms covalently bonded in 3D → very hard.
Recall graphite: layers with weak forces between layers → soft.
Thus diamond is harder.
misconceptions: Graphite is soft because it has weak C–C bonds. False; in-plane bonds are strong; interlayer forces are weak.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Diamond: 3D covalent → hard. Graphite: layered → soft.

flashcard.front: Why is diamond hard but graphite soft?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Diamond has 3D covalent network; graphite has weak interlayer forces.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is diamond the hardest natural substance?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Three-dimensional network of strong covalent bonds (sp³ hybridization).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show diamond and graphite structures visually.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain properties of allotropes of carbon
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Diamond has a rigid 3D network of covalent bonds (sp³), making it extremely hard.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Why does magnesium have a higher melting point than sodium?

options.A: Magnesium has more protons and neutrons.
options.B: Magnesium ions have a 2+ charge and twice the number of delocalized electrons, increasing metallic bond strength.
options.C: Sodium has stronger metallic bonds due to larger atomic radius.
options.D: Magnesium is a transition metal; sodium is not.

questions.hint: Compare the charge and number of valence electrons in Na vs Mg.
questions.explanation: Mg (Group 2) has two valence electrons and forms Mg²⁺ ions, while Na (Group 1) has one valence electron and forms Na⁺. The greater charge on Mg²⁺ and twice as many delocalized electrons create stronger metallic bonding, requiring more energy to melt.

source_trace.content_anchor: Group IIA metals are harder and higher melting than Group IA metals, because the IIA metals have closer packed structures and twice as many delocalized valence electrons.

explanations_tiered.definition: Melting point in metals depends on the strength of metallic bonding, which increases with ionic charge and number of delocalized electrons.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Mg²⁺ has a higher charge and twice the valence electrons of Na⁺, leading to stronger electrostatic attraction in the electron sea and a higher melting point.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Magnesium has twice the electrical 'glue' and stronger charges compared to sodium, so it takes more heat to melt.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Na has 1 valence e⁻ per atom; Mg has 2. Mg²⁺ also has a 2+ charge vs Na⁺ 1+. Both factors increase metallic bond strength, raising mp (Mg: 650°C, Na: 97.7°C).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The melting point of a metal correlates with the enthalpy of atomization, which depends on the number of delocalized electrons and the packing efficiency. The greater charge density of Mg²⁺ leads to stronger metallic bonding.
reasoning_steps: Recall that metallic bond strength increases with charge of cation and number of delocalized electrons.
Na: +1 charge, 1 delocalized e⁻. Mg: +2 charge, 2 delocalized e⁻.
Mg has stronger metallic bonds → higher melting point.
misconceptions: Higher atomic mass always means higher melting point. False; charge and electron count are more important.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Higher charge + more delocalized e⁻ = stronger metal bond = higher mp.

glossary_entry.definition: Temperature at which metallic bond breaks and solid becomes liquid.

flashcard.front: Why does Mg melt at a higher temperature than Na?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Mg²⁺ has double the charge and twice the delocalized electrons, strengthening the metallic bond.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is the melting point of Mg higher than Na?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Mg²⁺ has higher charge and more delocalized electrons, increasing metallic bond strength.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Higher charge + more e⁻ = stronger bond = higher mp.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare Be vs Li, Ca vs K for similar trends.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain melting point trends in metals
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Mg²⁺ has a 2+ charge and two delocalized electrons, forming stronger metallic bonds than Na⁺ with one electron.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.structural_anchor: Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds
questions.question: Which of the following ionic compounds would have the largest lattice energy (most negative)?
questions.hint: Lattice energy increases with higher charges and smaller ionic radii.
questions.explanation: Lattice energy ∝ (q₁ × q₂)/r. MgO has Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ (product 4), while NaCl and KBr have product 1. CaO also has product 4, but Mg²⁺ is smaller than Ca²⁺, so MgO has larger lattice energy.

source_trace.structural_element: Factors Affecting the Formation of Ionic Bonds

explanations_tiered.definition: Lattice energy increases with higher ion charges and smaller ion sizes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: MgO has +2 and −2 ions (high charge product) and small ions (small r), leading to very high lattice energy. NaCl and KBr have +1/−1. CaO has +2/−2 but larger r than MgO.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Lattice energy is like the strength of a magnet: stronger magnets (higher charge) and closer magnets (smaller ions) make a stronger bond. MgO wins on both.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using U ∝ (q₁q₂)/r: MgO: q₁=2, q₂=2, product=4, small r (~72 pm Mg²⁺, 140 pm O²⁻). CaO: product=4 but larger r (Ca²⁺ 100 pm). NaCl: product=1. Thus MgO has largest U.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Lattice energies (kJ/mol): MgO ~3795, CaO ~3414, NaCl ~787, KBr ~689. Charge product dominates, and within same charge product, smaller ionic radius increases U.
reasoning_steps: Identify ionic charges: NaCl (1+1-), MgO (2+2-), CaO (2+2-), KBr (1+1-).
MgO and CaO have higher charge product, so higher U.
Between MgO and CaO, Mg²⁺ is smaller than Ca²⁺ → MgO has smaller r → larger U.
misconceptions: Lattice energy depends only on charge, not radius. False; smaller ions increase U.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Highest U: high charge, small ions → MgO.

glossary_entry.definition: Energy released when gaseous ions form solid lattice.

flashcard.front: Which has higher lattice energy: MgO or CaO? Why?
flashcard.flashcard_back: MgO; Mg²⁺ is smaller than Ca²⁺, so distance is smaller, increasing U.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which compound has the largest lattice energy: NaCl, MgO, CaO, or KBr?
publishing_metadata.revision_note: MgO: high charges, small size → largest U.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use data table to show actual values.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare lattice energies of ionic compounds
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. MgO has 2+ and 2− charges (high charge product) and small ionic radii, giving the largest lattice energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the molecular shape of water (\(\mathrm{H_2O}\)) according to VSEPR theory?
questions.hint: Water has two bonding pairs and two lone pairs on oxygen.
questions.explanation: O in H₂O has four electron groups (2 bonds, 2 lone pairs). Electron geometry is tetrahedral, but the molecular shape is bent (V-shaped) because only two atoms are bonded to the central atom, and lone pairs are not counted in molecular shape.

source_trace.content_anchor: The geometry of H2O is bent

explanations_tiered.definition: Bent (V-shaped) geometry has a central atom with two bonded atoms and two lone pairs.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Four electron groups (2 bonds, 2 lone pairs) give tetrahedral electron geometry. The molecular shape is bent because only two atoms are attached.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Water looks like a bent V: the two hydrogens are the arms, and the oxygen is the center with two invisible lone pairs pushing the arms down.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: H₂O: O has 2 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs. Electron geometry = tetrahedral (109.5°), but molecular shape = bent (angle 104.5° due to lone pair repulsion).
explanations_tiered.advanced: VSEPR predicts bent geometry for AX₂E₂. The lone pairs occupy two tetrahedral positions, and the H atoms occupy the other two. The observed angle is 104.5°, less than ideal due to lone pair repulsion.
reasoning_steps: Draw Lewis structure of H₂O: O with two bonds, two lone pairs.
Count electron groups = 4 → tetrahedral electron geometry.
Only two atoms bonded → molecular shape = bent.
misconceptions: Water is tetrahedral. False; electron geometry is tetrahedral, but molecular shape is bent.

glossary_entry.definition: Molecular shape with two bonded atoms and one or two lone pairs on central atom.

flashcard.front: What is the molecular shape of water?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Bent (V-shaped) due to two lone pairs on oxygen.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Describe the molecular shape of H₂O.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Bent (V-shaped); 2 bonds + 2 lone pairs on oxygen.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Nonlinear shape due to lone pairs.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use VSEPR to determine molecular shape
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. H₂O has 2 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs, giving a bent molecular shape.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Using the MO diagram for homonuclear diatomic molecules (Z ≤ 8), calculate the bond order for \(\mathrm{N_2}\). Electronic configuration: \(\sigma_{1s}^2 \sigma_{1s}^{*2} \sigma_{2s}^2 \sigma_{2s}^{*2} \pi_{2p_x}^2 \pi_{2p_y}^2 \sigma_{2p_z}^2\).
questions.hint: Only valence electrons contribute to bond order; core electrons cancel.
questions.explanation: For N₂, core electrons (σ₁s²σ₁s*²) cancel (bonding − antibonding = 0). Valence: σ₂s²σ₂s*² (cancel), π₂p_x²π₂p_y² (bonding, 4 e⁻), σ₂p_z² (bonding, 2 e⁻). Bonding e⁻ = 6, antibonding = 0 (from valence), bond order = (6 − 0)/2 = 3.
formula_used: Bond order = \frac{1}{2}(\text{# bonding e⁻} - \text{# antibonding e⁻})

calculation_steps: Core electrons cancel (σ₁s²σ₁s*² = 0 net bond order contribution)

explanations_tiered.definition: Bond order is calculated from the number of bonding and antibonding electrons in molecular orbitals.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: N₂ has a triple bond (bond order 3): one σ and two π bonds from valence orbitals.
explanations_tiered.beginner: N₂ has one of the strongest bonds in chemistry. MO theory says it has a bond order of 3, which means a triple bond.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Valence electrons: σ₂s²σ₂s*² cancel (bond order 0). π₂p_x²π₂p_y² (4 bonding e⁻) and σ₂p_z² (2 bonding e⁻) give 6 bonding e⁻. Bond order = 6/2 = 3.
explanations_tiered.advanced: N₂'s high bond order (3) correlates with its short bond length (109.8 pm) and high dissociation energy (945 kJ/mol). The absence of antibonding valence electrons makes N₂ exceptionally stable.
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify core electrons (σ₁s²σ₁s*²) → net contribution 0.
Step 2: Identify valence bonding orbitals: σ₂s² (but canceled by σ₂s*²), π₂p_x², π₂p_y² (bonding), σ₂p_z² (bonding).
reasoning_steps: Write full MO configuration.
Cancel core electrons.
Cancel σ₂s²σ₂s*².
Count remaining bonding electrons.
Apply formula.
misconceptions: N₂ has a double bond. False; it has a triple bond (bond order 3).

glossary_entry.definition: Bond consisting of one σ and two π bonds, bond order = 3.

flashcard.front: What is the bond order of N₂ from MO theory?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate the bond order of N₂ from its MO configuration.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate bond order from MO diagrams

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Arrange the following hybridizations in order of increasing s-character: sp, sp², sp³.
questions.hint: s-character = (number of s orbitals / total number of hybrid orbitals) × 100%.
questions.explanation: sp: 1 s + 1 p → 50% s-character. sp²: 1 s + 2 p → 33% s-character. sp³: 1 s + 3 p → 25% s-character. Thus increasing s-character: sp³ (25%) < sp² (33%) < sp (50%).

source_trace.content_anchor: sp has 50% s-character, sp2 has 33%, sp3 has 25%

explanations_tiered.definition: s-character is the percentage of s orbital contribution in a hybrid orbital.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Higher s-character gives shorter, stronger bonds and larger bond angles. Order: sp (50%) > sp² (33%) > sp³ (25%).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a pizza: sp has half s (50%), sp² has one-third s (33%), sp³ has one-quarter s (25%).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: sp: 1 s out of 2 orbitals → 1/2 = 50% s. sp²: 1 s out of 3 → 33.3%. sp³: 1 s out of 4 → 25%. So increasing order: sp³ (25%) < sp² (33%) < sp (50%).
explanations_tiered.advanced: s-character affects electronegativity and bond strength. Higher s-character means electrons are held closer to the nucleus, influencing bond angles (e.g., sp³ 109.5°, sp² 120°, sp 180°).
reasoning_steps: Calculate s-character for sp: 1/2 = 50%.
Calculate for sp²: 1/3 ≈ 33%.
Calculate for sp³: 1/4 = 25%.
Arrange in ascending order: sp³ (25%) < sp² (33%) < sp (50%).
misconceptions: sp has the lowest s-character. False; sp has the highest s-character.

glossary_entry.definition: Fraction of s orbital contribution in a hybrid orbital.

flashcard.front: Rank sp, sp², sp³ by increasing s-character.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the order of increasing s-character among sp, sp², sp³?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to bond strength and angle.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. s-character: sp³ (25%) < sp² (33%) < sp (50%).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which statement best explains why solid sodium chloride does not conduct electricity, but molten sodium chloride does?

options.A: Covalent bonds in solid NaCl prevent conductivity; melting breaks covalent bonds.
options.B: In the solid, ions are fixed in lattice positions; in the liquid, ions are free to move.
options.C: Solid NaCl has no charged particles; molten NaCl produces electrons.
options.D: NaCl is a molecular solid; melting dissociates it into atoms.

questions.hint: Recall that ionic compounds conduct only when ions are mobile.
questions.explanation: In solid NaCl, ions are held in fixed positions by strong lattice energy and cannot move. When melted, the lattice breaks down, and ions become mobile, allowing them to carry electric charge.

source_trace.content_anchor: Ionic compounds conduct electricity when molten or dissolved because ions are free to move.

explanations_tiered.definition: Electrical conductivity requires mobile charged particles (ions or electrons).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Solid ionic compounds have fixed ions; molten ionic compounds have mobile ions, enabling conductivity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In solid salt, ions are stuck in place like cars in a traffic jam. Melt the salt, and ions can move like cars on a highway, carrying electricity.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: NaCl solid: Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions in fixed lattice sites cannot move. NaCl molten: lattice breaks, ions are mobile and can migrate to electrodes, conducting electricity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Conductivity in ionic compounds is electrolytic (ion migration). The high lattice energy of NaCl (786 kJ/mol) keeps ions fixed in solid; thermal energy overcomes lattice energy in molten state, freeing ions.
reasoning_steps: Identify that conductivity requires mobile charge carriers.
Solid: ions fixed in lattice → not mobile.
Molten: lattice disrupted → ions mobile → conductivity.
misconceptions: NaCl conducts in solid state because it has ions. False; ions must be mobile.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Solid ionic: no conductivity (fixed ions). Molten: conductivity (mobile ions).

glossary_entry.definition: Conduction by movement of ions in a liquid or solution.

flashcard.front: Why does molten NaCl conduct electricity but solid NaCl does not?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Ions are mobile in liquid but fixed in solid lattice.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does solid NaCl not conduct electricity?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Ions are fixed in the lattice and cannot move.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Ability of ions to move in a medium.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Solid ionic: fixed ions → no conductivity. Molten: mobile ions → conductivity.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Solid NaCl has fixed ions in lattice; molten NaCl has mobile ions that conduct electricity.
